reviews.why: True homonym. Child A's evidence (AAA transcript evA3) names a restaurant ordering-channel mix — delivery share of restaurant orders vs dine-in. Child B's evidence (BBB evB2, CCC evB3) names a parcel/courier shipment mix — ground vs air freight and next-day parcel services. Object differs (order channel vs shipment mode), scope differs (restaurants vs logistics), mechanism differs (consumer channel shift vs transport-mode/service shift) — all three union checks fail. New names use only evidence words: restaurant/delivery/channel/mix from evA3; parcel/delivery/mix from evB2+evB3.

Both occurrences name the same market-wide crude oil price as the cause. Object/metric: crude oil price in both quotes (evA4: "higher crude oil prices raised our distribution fuel costs"; evB4: "crude oil price increases pushed jet fuel and diesel costs higher"). Scope: the global commodity price, not a company- or segment-specific metric. Mechanism: oil price rise passes through as higher fuel input costs. The only difference is the downstream cost line affected (distribution fuel vs jet fuel/diesel), which is the effect on each company, not the reusable cause (DriverOntology R2). R5 lists oil_price as the canonical familiar name for exactly this driver.

Evidence is too thin to decide: each child has exactly 1 ref, and both quotes ("traffic was a factor this period" — AAA transcript evA6; "traffic trends were mixed" — BBB transcript evB5) never identify the object (store foot traffic vs web/site traffic vs network traffic), scope, or mechanism. The 3-check (same object/metric, same scope, same mechanism) is unverifiable on all three points, so SAME cannot be asserted. DIFFERENT also cannot be executed honestly: the quotes contain no qualifying nouns to build more-specific names from (only state words like "factor"/"mixed"/"trends", banned by DriverOntology R7), and there is no positive evidence the two usages diverge. Fail-close: park for more evidence.
